title: Zhao Lei Takes Stunning Pole in Rain-Hit Bahrain as Both Ferraris Crash Out in Q1
meta: Published: 11 April 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Zhao Lei claimed McLaren's first pole of 2026 by 0.003s at a chaotic, rain-soaked Bahrain qualifying.
body:
McLaren’s Zhao Lei took pole position for the Bahrain Grand Prix by the finest of margins — just 0.003 seconds — after a rain-hit qualifying session that turned into a nightmare for championship leader Eetu Väisänen and the entire Ferrari team. For the first time this season, no Ferrari will start in the top ten. Väisänen, winner of the first two grands prix, was eliminated in Q1, while his temporary teammate Riley Swansson, making his debut in red, could only manage 15th after a short-lived Q2 appearance. The result throws the championship battle wide open ahead of a Sunday race forecast to be dry and entirely different.

The session began in the most un-Bahrain like conditions imaginable: rare rain falling steadily, the track surface reflecting the cars as they emerged on intermediate tyres. DRS was initially disabled, and the early minutes of Q1 saw no one daring to go out. When the cars finally took to the track, the lap times hovered in the high 1:34s. But as the line dried and DRS was enabled, times began to fall. Lei Zhao, who had run wide at Turn 7 on his first flying lap, recovered to set a competitive time, but the real drama was unfolding at the bottom of the order.

With four minutes remaining, a dry line was forming, but the rain persisted, making the track a patchwork of grip and slipperiness. Swansson, on his first qualifying run for Ferrari, crossed the line just in time to bump Philippe Verhaeren’s Red Bull into the drop zone. Jack Tef, the Williams team principal who replaced the injured Lucas Koopman, was knocked out in 17th, alongside Julian Kondra and Afonso Cachinho — the latter now driving for Haas after the temporary driver swap. But the shock of the session was the elimination of Eetu Väisänen. The championship leader, who had started the season with two wins from the opening three races, could not extract enough grip from his Ferrari, which had arrived with no upgrades, and was caught out by the rapidly evolving conditions. It was a mistake that could prove costly in the championship fight.

Q2 brought a brief respite as the rain eased, and a dry line began to appear. Richard Hicks set an early benchmark on intermediates, but as the track dried further, teams faced a tyre gamble. Eren Aygen was the first to try soft slicks, but the rain returned just as he started his lap, and his gamble failed. Jason Roy and Lei Zhao then traded fastest times, both dipping into the 1:22s, before the rain intensified again, halting any further improvements. When the chequered flag fell, both Ferraris were out: Swansson in 15th, and Väisänen already gone. For the first time since the start of the season, neither scarlet car would feature in the pole shootout.

Q3 was delayed by five minutes as the rain worsened, and DRS remained disabled for the entire session. In these treacherous conditions, drivers had one chance to set a lap before a sandstorm-like gloom descended over the circuit. Richard Hicks went first, his Audi setting a 1:35.110. Robin Keisner spun on his attempt, ruining not only his own lap but also that of Sarah Springfield, who was following closely behind. Then came Lei Zhao, who threaded together a clean lap despite the worsening visibility to set a 1:34.973. Jason Roy’s Red Bull crossed the line just 0.003 seconds slower — the tightest margin of the season. Quarter Kevu put his Racing Bulls car third, a further 0.055 seconds back, with Hicks holding onto fourth. Michiii MaraDöner, Lukas Wright, Joshua Nathanial, Keisner, and Springfield completed the top nine, all covered by less than seven tenths.

The result is a dramatic shake-up of the grid for tomorrow’s race. Lei Zhao, who has been quietly accumulating points all season, now has a golden opportunity to close the gap to Väisänen at the top of the championship. Jason Roy, starting alongside, will be equally hungry to convert his best qualifying of the year into a strong result. And both Ferraris — starting from the lower reaches of the grid — will need a masterclass in strategy and racecraft to salvage points from a weekend that has so far gone disastrously wrong. Bahrain, so often a predictable opener, has delivered a qualifying session that will be remembered for its chaos, its fine margins, and the spectacular fall of the sport’s dominant force.
